title: Rare events of small-noise Doob conditioned processes
abstract: Doob fixed-time conditioning enables the sampling of rare trajectories of Markov processes by modifying the drift so that reaching a prescribed target at a given time is guaranteed. We study the statistics of this conditioned path ensemble through the moment generating function in the weak-noise large deviation regime. Since the Doob drift is rarely available in closed form, we reinterpret the conditioned ensemble as the original process post-selected on the terminal constraint, thereby avoiding explicit construction of the Doob transform. This viewpoint then yields an optimal-control representation for the leading exponential contribution to the generating function, expressed as a variational principle with terminal boundary conditions set by the Doob end-point constraint. We illustrate the framework with two analytical examples and with an application to heat dissipation of a minimal model of biomolecular folding.
summary: This paper provides a new way to study rare trajectories in noisy systems by avoiding the complex math of Doob transforms through a post-selection approach. It allows for the calculation of fluctuations in quantities like heat dissipation using an optimal-control framework. The method is validated through analytical models and applied to complex biomolecular landscapes.
main_problem: Calculating the statistics and moment generating functions of path observables for Doob-conditioned processes in the small-noise, large-deviation regime.
main_result: The authors develop a framework to bypass the need for an explicit Doob h-function by treating the conditioned ensemble as a post-selected original process, yielding an optimal-control representation for the action.
method: The study uses weak-noise large deviation theory, a WKBJ ansatz, and a Hamilton-Jacobi formulation to derive a variational principle for the leading exponential contribution to the generating function.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to time-homogeneous diffusion processes (SDEs) with additive noise, specifically using Brownian bridges, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes, and a 2D biomolecular folding model.
key_observables: Moment generating functions, Scaled Cumulant Generating Function (SCGF), rate functions, dissipated heat, and accumulated area under the path.
important_parameters: Noise intensity (epsilon), terminal time (T), tilting parameter (s), and diffusion coefficient (D).
main_assumptions: The noise is primarily assumed to be additive, the processes are Markovian and time-homogeneous, and the study focuses on the small-noise (epsilon -> 0) limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Doob conditioning concept; Figure 2 compares analytical and numerical optimal trajectories and area rates for Brownian bridges; Figure 3 shows OU process trajectories, heat dissipation, and SCGF; Figure 4 presents numerical results for a 2D folding model including instantons and heat dissipation.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of Doob conditioning and the difficulty of the h-transform, introduces a post-selection reinterpretation, develops the mathematical framework via Hamilton-Jacobi and optimal control theory, provides analytical benchmarks (Brownian bridge and OU process), and concludes with a numerical application to a biomolecular folding landscape.
why_interesting: The development of a variational principle for conditioned trajectories is highly relevant for studying rare fluctuations and non-equilibrium transitions in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
